title: Sum a Decayed Array
prompt_md:
## Goal
A C-style array decays to a pointer when passed to a function, losing its length -- so the length must travel as a separate argument. Write a function that sums a decayed `int` array given a pointer and a length, and call it from `main`.

## Input
- Line 1: an integer `n` (1 <= n <= 10).
- Line 2: `n` integers (each from -1000 to 1000).

## Output
One line: `sum: <total>`.

Do not use `std::array`, `std::vector`, or any container.

## Example
Input `4` then `10 20 30 40` produces `sum: 100`.

test_cases.label: Sample -- small array with positive values
Hidden -- mixed positive and negative values

title: Find the Maximum via Decayed Array
prompt_md:
## Goal
Demonstrate why a decayed array must carry its length. In `main`, the C-style array has **not** decayed, so you can compute its element count with `sizeof(arr) / sizeof(arr[0])`. The moment you pass the array to a function it decays to a plain pointer and that length is gone -- so you must pass the length you computed as a separate argument.

Fill the whole `int arr[10]` from input, derive the length in `main` with `sizeof`, and pass the decayed array plus that length to a `findMax(const int* arr, int length)` function. Initialize your running maximum from the first element (do not use `<limits>`).

## Input
A single line containing exactly 10 integers (each from -1000 to 1000).

## Output
One line: `max: <maximum>` -- the largest of the 10 values.

The maximum must be produced by scanning `length` elements inside `findMax`, where `length` is the `sizeof`-derived count from `main`. Do not use `std::array`, `std::vector`, or `<algorithm>`.

## Example
Input `5 12 8 3 20 1 7 9 4 6` produces `max: 20`.
